Builder's brief & quote request

Refurbishing our family house at Simon

A single-storey family house in Simon, St Andrew, being converted into a sleeps-eight villa — four bedrooms (keeping the existing walls) and a vaulted great room that opens to the garden. There is a draughtsman's set to work from. This page has everything you need to price the work — and how to send us your quote.

Where
Simon, St Andrew
The house
Single-storey, approx. 125 m²
The owner
UK-based, works with you remotely
On-site contact

A quick hello from Rob — 20 secondsWatch on YouTube
1

Who we are & how this works

We're the Berkeley family. The house sits on our seven-acre family land at Simon and we're refurbishing it so it can be let to visitors. The owner, Rob, is based in the United Kingdom and will work with you by phone and WhatsApp; a family member on the island can let you in and be your day-to-day point of contact.

We're looking for one reliable builder to price and carry out the work below. We have a draughtsman's set to work from (the Option A plans — linked in the next section). Building permits and planning approval are our responsibility, not yours. Please read this page, then send us an itemised quote — there's a template at the end. If anything is unclear, tell us; good questions now save everyone money later.

2

The work we want done

We're licensing this as a villa (whole-house let), not a guest house — so there's no minimum room-size rule, and we're keeping almost all the existing walls. The ambition goes into opening the house to the garden and cooling it naturally, not into moving walls. Please quote it itemised, and split essential-to-open from later-phase.

Layout — keep the walls, sleeps 8

  • No bedroom reconfiguration. Keep the three existing bedrooms as they are — as a villa there's no minimum size. Refresh only.
  • Fourth bedroom: convert the living room to a bedroom by fitting a door in the existing opening (it already has a verandah door and two windows). That takes us to sleeps 8.
  • Bunk room: a built-in bunk (guard rail, ladder, reading light per berth) in the smallest bedroom — a feature, not a squeeze.
  • Only internal wall removed: the laundry wall. The laundry itself is kept.
  • Square off three internal arches: three arched openings inside the house to be squared off to clean rectangular openings (see photos). Keep head heights consistent across them; make good the reveals, lintels and finishes.

The one structural move — open the great room to the garden

  • Form an approx. 5 m opening in the rear wall of the vaulted great room (kitchen / dining / living) for impact-rated sliding doors, on a structural beam + posts (engineer to size) for gravity and hurricane uplift. This is the one big structural item — the indoor-outdoor hero.
  • A deck landing off the opening for now (the full deck comes in a later phase — see below).

Cool without air-conditioning

  • No AC. Cool by design — ceiling fans throughout and maximum cross-ventilation.
  • Windows: replace with louvre / awning units for airflow, behind decorative security-mesh grilles — open to breathe, secure at night. Quick-release egress to every bedroom (secure from outside, openable from inside without a key — never a fixed bar over a sleeping-room window).
  • Bahama shutters to the openings — shade, storm protection, and a lock-down skin when the house is empty.
  • Kitchen roof: a section of opal / heat-control multiwall polycarbonate for daylight without the heat — top-vented.
  • Skylights: operable, low-e, impact-rated, with blackout blinds to the sleeping rooms.
  • The enclosed stairwell as a cooling chimney: louvre the kitchen into it and make sure it exhausts at the top; a rope bannister along the wall; replace the door with a lockable security gate (quick-release if it's an escape route). Trace and fix the damp under the stairs — find the source, don't just patch.

Bathrooms — the two we have, working harder

  • Keep the two existing toilets — no third bathroom. Compartmentalise so at least one WC is its own cubicle (never trapped behind a running shower); twin basins in the shared vanity.
  • In-room washbasins in the master (and its neighbour if the plumbed corner reaches) — takes the wash traffic out of the bathroom. Tiled splashback, hot and cold, waste to the stack.
  • Refresh both bathrooms — new fittings, tiling, proper waterproofing, and extraction to control humidity and mould.

Services, safety & the fabric

  • Full rewire — the old two-meter supply likely has no earth. New consumer unit with RCD, proper earthing, and make it solar-ready (we're fitting solar PV + battery + solar hot water).
  • Water: service and assess the cistern and pump; quote more storage if needed.
  • Damp & cracks: repair the kitchen-wall and bedroom-window cracks and any damp — distinguish structural from cosmetic in the quote.
  • Roof: assess honestly. If it's sound, make good + hurricane tie-downs + solar-ready. Only quote a full standing-seam re-roof if it genuinely needs replacing — flag it separately.
  • Septic / soakaway: probe and confirm before we sign; flag any work.
  • Fire / life-safety: smoke + heat detectors, extinguisher, emergency lighting (our solar covers this), clear escape routes — the tourism / fire inspection will check.
  • Fine-mesh insect screens to all openings and vents; external / security lighting; a lockable housekeeping store.

The outdoor amenity — phased

  • For opening: a hot tub on the master-suite deck — supply and site it with its deck, electrics and plumbing. This is the Phase-0 water amenity.
  • Later, from cashflow (quote separately so we can plan it): the full plunge pool, the full deck, and the outdoor kitchen.

How to quote it

  • Itemised — materials and labour separated where you can.
  • Essential-to-open vs nice-to-have / later phase clearly split. We're opening lean and adding from earnings.

4

Questions you might have

The things builders usually ask us — answered up front.

Who supplies the materials — you or us?

Please quote to supply both materials and labour, shown separately. If you'd rather we buy certain items ourselves, tell us which.

Who supplies fixtures, fittings and appliances?

Tell us what you'd include and what you'd prefer we buy. We're happy to purchase big items — fridge, cooker, the AC units — if that's simpler.

Is there water and power on site during the work?

Grid power (220V / 50Hz) is at the property, though outages are common — please confirm the meter is active and factor interruptions into your programme. Water: to confirm.

Who arranges permits and the tourism licence?

Building permits and planning approval are our responsibility, not yours. The finished house must meet Grenada Tourism Authority standards for an Accommodation Licence (fire, health, sanitation, structural safety) — and that licence is needed before we can take any guests, so please build to pass GTA inspection first time. Materials imported for the project qualify for GIDC duty exemption only if each bedroom is at least 23.2 m² — a hard requirement for our layout. Tell us if any of your work needs a trade licence or inspection you'd normally arrange.

Will the rainy season affect the work?

Likely yes (Jun–Nov). Please tell us how you handle weather delays and protect fresh concrete and open work from the rain.

Where does the rubble and waste go?

Please include removal and disposal in your price, and tell us how you handle it.

Is anyone living in the house while you work?

No — the house will be empty during the works, so you can work freely.

Who's on site day-to-day, and who lets me in?

Claudia, a neighbour — 536 1397 — can give you access and be your local point of contact. The owner is in the UK and reachable on WhatsApp.

How will we communicate with an owner overseas?

WhatsApp for quick things, and a short weekly update with photos or a video walkthrough works well. We make decisions quickly, so you won't be left waiting.

What currency should I quote in?

Quote in EC$ (XCD). GBP or US$ are also fine — just tell us which you've used.

How do you want to pay?

We prefer a staged schedule tied to milestones. We understand you may need a deposit to buy materials — if so, show us the supplier quotes or receipts and we'll cover materials directly or agree a fair deposit. We just don't pay large sums with nothing to show.

When could you start, and how long would it take?

Tell us your earliest realistic start date and how long the work would take. We know good builders are in high demand right now, so honesty helps us plan.

What if something's wrong after you finish?

Tell us your guarantee — how long it runs, and how you handle putting snags right.

5

How to send us your quote

Please send an itemised quote that covers:

  • A price for each part of the work — grouped as on this page, with the optional outdoor additions kept separate.
  • Confirmation that each bedroom achieves at least 23.2 m².
  • Labour and materials shown separately where you can.
  • What's included and what's excluded.
  • Your proposed payment schedule.
  • Your earliest start date and how long the work will take.
  • How long the quote is valid for, and any assumptions you've made.
Send by WhatsApp Send by email

The email button opens a ready-made template you can fill in.

6

About your firm

We want a builder we can trust from a distance, so please tell us a little about you. This matters as much to us as the price.

  • How long you've been building, and your business name and registration (BIR) number.
  • Similar jobs you've done — renovations, and especially any villa, guesthouse or holiday-let work.
  • Your experience with concrete-block construction in coastal / high-humidity conditions.
  • Two or three references we can call — a name, what you did for them, and a phone number. Recent, completed jobs are best.
  • A few photographs of finished work.
  • Your team — which trades are in-house and what you subcontract. Electrical and plumbing work must be done or signed off by Grenada-licensed tradespeople; please confirm who holds the licences.
  • Whether you own or rent the scaffolding, concrete mixer and compaction equipment this job needs.
  • Whether you carry liability insurance.
  • Any experience working with owners who live overseas.
  • The guarantee you offer on your work.
